Temporal Patterns.pattern_name: Sharp year-over-year step-up in transaction volume in 2021
Temporal Patterns.description: Monthly record counts rise sharply from 2020 to 2021 and remain consistently high throughout 2021.
Temporal Patterns.evidence: Counts are 60-150 per month in 2020 (for example, 150 in 2020-01, 60 in 2020-06, 92 in 2020-12) versus 662-728 per month in 2021 (711 in 2021-01, 725 in 2021-05, 728 in 2021-09 and 2021-10, 667 in 2021-12). This is a clear level shift beginning in 2021-01.
Temporal Patterns.business_relevance: Any time-based comparison of sums between 2020 and 2021 is heavily influenced by much higher transaction volume in 2021.

Temporal Patterns.pattern_name: Total Sales sums are much higher in every month of 2021 than the same month in 2020
Temporal Patterns.description: Monthly Total Sales show a clear year-over-year increase across the full calendar year.
Temporal Patterns.business_relevance: Revenue expanded materially year over year, indicating a much larger sales base in 2021.

Temporal Patterns.pattern_name: Operating Profit sums are much higher in every month of 2021 than the same month in 2020
Temporal Patterns.description: Monthly Operating Profit follows the same year-over-year expansion pattern as Total Sales.
Temporal Patterns.business_relevance: Profitability in absolute dollars increased substantially year over year.

Temporal Patterns.pattern_name: Units Sold sums are much higher in every month of 2021 than the same month in 2020
Temporal Patterns.description: Monthly unit volume increases consistently from 2020 to 2021 across all matched months.
Temporal Patterns.business_relevance: The revenue and profit increase is accompanied by substantially higher unit movement.

Temporal Patterns.pattern_name: 2020 mid-year decline followed by partial recovery in Total Sales
Temporal Patterns.description: In 2020, Total Sales fall sharply from spring into early summer, recover in late summer, then weaken again toward year-end.
Temporal Patterns.business_relevance: 2020 shows pronounced within-year volatility, which may matter for planning and benchmarking.

Temporal Patterns.pattern_name: 2021 Total Sales dip in Q1, strengthen through summer, soften in fall, rebound in December
Temporal Patterns.description: In 2021, Total Sales decline from January to March, then rise strongly into July, fall through October, and recover in November-December.
Temporal Patterns.business_relevance: This indicates a clear 2021 seasonal shape with summer strength, autumn softness, and year-end rebound.

Temporal Patterns.pattern_name: 2021 Operating Profit follows the same seasonal shape as Total Sales
Temporal Patterns.description: Operating Profit in 2021 declines in Q1, climbs into summer, weakens in fall, and rebounds strongly in December.
Temporal Patterns.business_relevance: Profit timing closely tracks revenue timing, useful for cash flow and earnings planning.

Temporal Patterns.pattern_name: 2021 Units Sold show summer peak and fall dip
Temporal Patterns.description: Unit volume in 2021 is lowest in early spring, rises into late summer, then declines in autumn before recovering in December.
Temporal Patterns.business_relevance: Demand volume appears strongest in summer and weaker in autumn.

Temporal Patterns.pattern_name: Price per Unit mean is lower in early 2021 than in 2020, then trends upward through late 2021
Temporal Patterns.description: Average selling price is relatively high in parts of 2020, drops to its lowest levels in early 2021, and then rises steadily toward year-end 2021.
Temporal Patterns.business_relevance: Late-2021 revenue gains were supported not only by volume but also by higher average unit prices.

Temporal Patterns.pattern_name: Average Units Sold per record are higher in 2020 than in 2021
Temporal Patterns.description: Despite higher total unit volume in 2021, the mean Units Sold per record is generally lower than in 2020 because record counts are much higher.
Temporal Patterns.evidence: 2020 mean Units Sold per record often ranges from 322.22 to 480.47, including 420.84 in 2020-04, 448.25 in 2020-08, and 480.47 in 2020-09. In 2021, monthly means range from 209.94 to 273.69, including 214.59 in 2021-03, 209.94 in 2021-10, and a high of 273.69 in 2021-08.
Temporal Patterns.business_relevance: Growth in 2021 came from many more records rather than larger average unit size per record.

Temporal Patterns.pattern_name: Average Total Sales per record are higher in 2020 than in 2021
Temporal Patterns.description: Mean Total Sales per record are mostly higher in 2020, while 2021 has larger monthly sums driven by more records.
Temporal Patterns.evidence: 2020 mean Total Sales per record include 189,284.66 in 2020-04 and 190,671.21 in 2020-09, with most months above 100,000 except 2020-11 and 2020-12. In 2021, means are lower, ranging from 58,343.56 in 2021-03 to 116,917.43 in 2021-07 and 116,664.81 in 2021-12.
Temporal Patterns.business_relevance: Per-transaction revenue appears smaller in 2021 even though total monthly revenue is much larger.

Temporal Patterns.pattern_name: Average Operating Profit per record is higher in 2020 than in most of 2021 months
Temporal Patterns.description: Per-record profit is generally higher in 2020, while 2021 profit growth comes from scale rather than higher profit per record.
Temporal Patterns.evidence: 2020 mean Operating Profit per record reaches 71,548.48 in 2020-04 and 74,163.61 in 2020-09. In 2021, monthly means are lower, ranging from 21,725.48 in 2021-03 to 43,488.44 in 2021-07 and 43,295.41 in 2021-12.
Temporal Patterns.business_relevance: Operational expansion in 2021 increased total profit, but average profit per record was lower.

Temporal Patterns.pattern_name: Retailer ID mean is stable over time
Temporal Patterns.description: The monthly mean of Retailer ID changes very little across the full period, indicating no meaningful temporal trend in this aggregated measure.
Temporal Patterns.business_relevance: This field does not show a useful time trend in its monthly mean and is likely not informative for seasonality analysis.

Correlation Patterns.pattern_name: Total Sales and Operating Profit move together very strongly
Correlation Patterns.description: Total Sales and Operating Profit have a very strong positive relationship: records with higher sales tend to also show higher operating profit. This is likely largely structural because profit is financially tied to sales, though the less-than-perfect correlation indicates margins or cost structure vary across records.
Correlation Patterns.evidence: Pearson correlation r = 0.9563, positive direction. As Total Sales increases, Operating Profit also tends to increase strongly.
Correlation Patterns.business_relevance: This suggests revenue growth is closely associated with profit growth in the dataset. From a business perspective, sales expansion appears to translate into higher operating profit, but not perfectly, implying margin differences across products, channels, or transactions.

Correlation Patterns.pattern_name: Units Sold strongly co-moves with Total Sales
Correlation Patterns.description: Units Sold and Total Sales show a strong positive relationship: transactions with more units sold generally produce higher total sales. This relationship is likely structural because sales revenue is directly influenced by quantity sold, together with price per unit.
Correlation Patterns.evidence: Pearson correlation r = 0.9134, positive direction. Higher Units Sold is associated with higher Total Sales.
Correlation Patterns.business_relevance: Volume is a major driver of revenue in this dataset. Increasing units sold is strongly linked to higher sales outcomes, which is useful for forecasting and for identifying volume-led growth opportunities.

Correlation Patterns.pattern_name: Units Sold strongly co-moves with Operating Profit
Correlation Patterns.description: Units Sold and Operating Profit have a strong positive relationship: records with higher sales volume tend to generate higher operating profit. This is partly structural because more units sold tends to raise sales, which in turn supports profit, though profitability per unit is not constant.
Correlation Patterns.evidence: Pearson correlation r = 0.8924, positive direction. Higher Units Sold is associated with higher Operating Profit.
Correlation Patterns.business_relevance: Sales volume appears to be an important profit driver. Operationally, this means initiatives that increase unit throughput are likely to improve profit, although the correlation being lower than Sales-Profit suggests margins and pricing still matter.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: Retailer transaction volume is concentrated in three retailers
Grouping Patterns.description: Foot Locker, West Gear, and Sports Direct dominate the dataset by record count, while Walmart and Amazon have much smaller representation.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Retailer counts are Foot Locker 2,637, West Gear 2,374, Sports Direct 2,032, Kohl's 1,030, Amazon 949, and Walmart 626. Foot Locker has 4.2× as many records as Walmart (2,637 vs 626). The top three retailers together account for 7,043 of 9,648 records, about 73.0% of all retailer-grouped records.
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: High. Any retailer-level analysis or planning will be heavily influenced by these three retailers, so they should be prioritized for segmentation, forecasting, and partnership decisions.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: West Gear and Foot Locker lead total units sold, but Walmart leads per-record unit volume
Grouping Patterns.description: The dominant retailers differ depending on whether total volume or average volume per record is considered.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Total units sold are West Gear 625,262, Foot Locker 604,369, Sports Direct 557,640, Kohl's 287,375, Walmart 206,225, and Amazon 197,990. However, mean units sold per record are highest for Walmart at 329.43, followed by Kohl's 279.00, Sports Direct 274.43, West Gear 263.38, Foot Locker 229.19, and Amazon 208.63. Walmart's average is 1.58× Amazon's (329.43 vs 208.63), despite Walmart having only 626 records versus Amazon's 949.
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: High. West Gear and Foot Locker matter most for aggregate volume, while Walmart appears to generate larger orders per record, which may call for different inventory and channel strategies.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: Amazon has the highest average price per unit among retailers
Grouping Patterns.description: Retailer pricing levels are uneven, with Amazon at the top and Sports Direct at the bottom on average price per unit.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Mean price per unit by retailer is Amazon 48.76, Walmart 47.18, West Gear 46.74, Foot Locker 44.78, Kohl's 44.61, and Sports Direct 42.05. Amazon's average price is 6.71 higher than Sports Direct's (48.76 vs 42.05), about 1.16× higher.
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: Moderate to high. This indicates retailer-level pricing differences that may affect positioning, margin expectations, and promotional planning.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: Regional record counts are concentrated in West and Northeast
Grouping Patterns.description: The West and Northeast have the largest number of records, while the Southeast has the smallest.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Region counts are West 2,448, Northeast 2,376, Midwest 1,872, South 1,728, and Southeast 1,224. The West has exactly 2.0× the Southeast's record count (2,448 vs 1,224). West and Northeast together account for 4,824 of 9,648 records, exactly 50.0% of the total.
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: High. Coverage, staffing, and market analysis will be disproportionately driven by West and Northeast activity.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: Southeast has the highest average units sold per record despite the fewest records
Grouping Patterns.description: The Southeast is underrepresented in record count but leads all regions in average units sold per record.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: The Southeast has the fewest records at 1,224, but the highest mean units sold at 332.52. Other regional means are South 284.87, West 280.63, Northeast 210.98, and Midwest 209.05. Southeast's mean is 1.59× Midwest's (332.52 vs 209.05).
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: High. The Southeast appears to produce larger transactions on average, which is important for inventory allocation and sales strategy even though it has fewer records.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: West dominates total regional units sold
Grouping Patterns.description: The West is the largest region by total units sold, well ahead of the Midwest and clearly above all other regions.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Total units sold by region are West 686,985, Northeast 501,279, South 492,260, Southeast 407,000, and Midwest 391,337. The West exceeds the Midwest by 295,648 units (686,985 vs 391,337), or about 1.76×.
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: High. The West is the largest volume region and should be a priority for supply, fulfillment, and demand planning.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: Higher-price regions are West and Southeast; lower-price regions are South and Midwest
Grouping Patterns.description: Regional pricing is uneven, with a clear split between higher-price and lower-price regions.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Mean price per unit is Southeast 50.18, West 49.94, Northeast 46.69, Midwest 40.36, and South 38.24. The Southeast average is 11.95 higher than the South (50.18 vs 38.24), about 1.31× higher.
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: High. Regional pricing differences are large enough to matter for pricing policy, margin management, and promotion design.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: A few states dominate record counts
Grouping Patterns.description: State-level representation is uneven, with Texas and California having the largest counts, followed by Florida and New York.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Texas and California each have 432 records, Florida and New York each have 360, Charleston's state-level equivalent is not applicable, and many states have only 144 or 216. Texas and California each have 3.0× the record count of a 144-record state such as Illinois or Ohio (432 vs 144).
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: High. State-level analyses will be disproportionately influenced by Texas and California, so these markets deserve separate attention.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: New York, California, Texas, and Florida lead states in total units sold
Grouping Patterns.description: A small set of large states accounts for the highest total unit volumes.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: State total units sold are New York 169,140, California 163,284, Texas 159,500, and Florida 133,430. These are well above lower-volume states such as Nebraska 19,154 and Maryland 20,818. New York sells 8.83× Nebraska's total units (169,140 vs 19,154).
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: High. These states are the main volume drivers and should be prioritized for inventory, marketing, and performance tracking.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: South Carolina and New York stand out on average units sold per state
Grouping Patterns.description: Some states have unusually high average units sold per record compared with the broader state distribution.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Mean units sold per record are highest in South Carolina 504.24 and New York 469.83, followed by North Carolina 437.06, California 377.97, Florida 370.64, and Texas 369.21. South Carolina's mean is 3.97× Rhode Island's 127.19 and 3.79× Nebraska's 133.01.
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: High. These states appear to generate much larger transactions on average, which can affect stocking, logistics, and account management.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: State pricing is highly uneven, with Seattle/Washington and Hawaii at the top and Utah at the bottom
Grouping Patterns.description: Average price per unit varies widely across states, indicating a broad spread in pricing levels.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Highest mean prices include Washington 67.65, Hawaii 64.25, and Colorado 60.09, while the lowest include Utah 26.83, Missouri 31.49, Arkansas 33.34, and Oklahoma 33.42. Washington's mean price is 2.52× Utah's (67.65 vs 26.83).
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: High. The spread is large enough to matter for regional pricing, assortment decisions, and margin analysis.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: City-level volume is concentrated in a small set of cities
Grouping Patterns.description: A few cities account for the highest total units sold, while many cities are much smaller.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Top city totals include New York 111,954, Charleston 102,483, Houston 90,322, San Francisco 86,900, Los Angeles 76,384, and Miami 73,135. New York's total is 5.84× Omaha's 19,154 and 4.03× Philadelphia's 27,662.
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: High. These cities are major demand centers and are likely the most important for localized inventory and sales planning.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: New York and Miami have exceptionally high average units sold per city record
Grouping Patterns.description: Average transaction size varies sharply across cities, with New York and Miami clearly above most others.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Mean units sold per record are New York 518.31, Miami 507.88, Charlotte 437.06, Houston 418.16, San Francisco 402.31, and Albany 397.13. These compare with much lower cities such as Providence 127.19, Philadelphia 128.06, Omaha 133.01, and Baltimore 144.57. New York's mean is 4.08× Providence's (518.31 vs 127.19).
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: High. Cities with very high per-record volume may need different replenishment and service models than low-volume cities.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: Product record counts are nearly uniform across categories
Grouping Patterns.description: Unlike geography and retailer, product categories are very evenly represented by count.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Product counts range narrowly from 1,606 to 1,610: Men's Apparel 1,606, Men's Athletic Footwear 1,610, Men's Street Footwear 1,610, Women's Apparel 1,608, Women's Athletic Footwear 1,606, Women's Street Footwear 1,608. The maximum difference is only 4 records.
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: Moderate. Product comparisons are less likely to be distorted by unequal sample sizes than retailer or geography comparisons.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: Apparel categories have higher prices than footwear categories
Grouping Patterns.description: Average price per unit is consistently higher for apparel than for footwear, especially versus women's footwear.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Mean price per unit is Women's Apparel 51.60 and Men's Apparel 50.32, compared with Men's Athletic Footwear 43.78, Men's Street Footwear 44.24, Women's Athletic Footwear 41.11, and Women's Street Footwear 40.25. Women's Apparel is 11.35 higher than Women's Street Footwear (51.60 vs 40.25), about 1.28×.
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: High. This indicates a clear product-segment pricing split that matters for assortment, margin expectations, and promotion strategy.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: Men's Street Footwear is the dominant product by units sold
Grouping Patterns.description: Among product categories, Men's Street Footwear leads clearly in both total units sold and average units sold per record.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Men's Street Footwear has total units sold of 593,320 and mean units sold of 368.52, both highest among products. The next-highest totals are Men's Athletic Footwear 435,526 and Women's Apparel 433,827. Men's Street Footwear total is 1.93× Men's Apparel's 306,683, and its mean is 1.88× Men's Apparel's 190.96.
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: High. This category is the clearest product-volume leader and should be central to inventory, merchandising, and demand planning.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: Women's Athletic Footwear and Men's Apparel are lower-volume product segments
Grouping Patterns.description: Some product categories lag meaningfully behind the leaders in both total and average units sold.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Lowest total units sold are Men's Apparel 306,683 and Women's Athletic Footwear 317,236. Their mean units sold are also low at 190.96 and 197.53, compared with Men's Street Footwear at 368.52 and Men's Athletic Footwear at 270.51. Men's Street Footwear's average is 1.87× Women's Athletic Footwear's (368.52 vs 197.53).
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: Moderate to high. These segments may require different promotional or assortment strategies if the goal is to lift volume.

Anomaly Patterns.pattern_name: Heavy right-tail outliers in Operating Profit
Anomaly Patterns.description: Operating Profit shows a pronounced concentration of unusually high values relative to its central range, indicating a heavy right tail.
Anomaly Patterns.evidence: 706 outliers were flagged (7.3176%), the highest outlier rate among the measured business metrics. The IQR-based upper bound is 127274.5, while the maximum is 390000. Mean is 34425.28 versus median 4371.5, a very large gap. Skewness is 2.2336 and kurtosis is 5.8204, both indicating a strong right tail and extreme-value concentration.
Anomaly Patterns.business_relevance: This is more consistent with real business concentration in a subset of records than random noise, because multiple statistics align in the same direction. Downstream analysis should use robust summaries (median, IQR), consider winsorization or log transforms for modeling, and separately inspect the highest-profit records to confirm they are valid.

Anomaly Patterns.pattern_name: Extreme right-skew and high-value spikes in Total Sales
Anomaly Patterns.description: Total Sales contains many unusually large records compared with the typical transaction level, producing a strongly skewed distribution with substantial upper-end spikes.
Anomaly Patterns.evidence: 653 outliers were flagged (6.7682%). The upper outlier bound is 368618.25, while the maximum reaches 825000. Mean is 93273.44 but median is only 9576.0, showing the average is heavily pulled upward by large values. The 75th percentile is 150000.0 versus the 25th percentile of 4254.5, indicating a very wide spread. Skewness is 1.8414 and kurtosis is 3.1438.
Anomaly Patterns.business_relevance: These values may reflect real large sales events or aggregation differences rather than obvious data errors, since the minimum is 0 and no negative sales are present. Use robust statistics, segment analyses by sales size, and validate whether very large sales records represent bulk orders or a different transaction grain.

Anomaly Patterns.pattern_name: Right-skewed Units Sold with upper-end volume outliers
Anomaly Patterns.description: Units Sold has a long upper tail with a noticeable set of unusually high-volume records.
Anomaly Patterns.evidence: 508 outliers were flagged (5.2653%). The upper outlier bound is 716.0, while the maximum is 1275. Mean is 256.93 versus median 176.0. The distribution has skewness 1.4623 and kurtosis 1.6997, supporting a right-skewed shape with elevated high-end values. Minimum is 0.0, so zero-unit records also exist.
Anomaly Patterns.business_relevance: High-unit records are likely real high-volume sales events, but they can dominate averages and regression fits. Downstream analysis should consider robust scaling, possible log transformation, and separate review of zero-unit and very high-unit records.

Anomaly Patterns.pattern_name: Zero-valued records in sales and profit measures
Anomaly Patterns.description: The dataset contains records with zero Total Sales, zero Operating Profit, and zero Units Sold, which is unusual for completed sale records and may indicate cancellations, placeholders, or nonstandard transaction states.
Anomaly Patterns.evidence: Minimum values are 0.0 for Units Sold, 0.0 for Total Sales, and 0.0 for Operating Profit. Because these are transactional performance fields, exact zeros across these measures are notable. No negative values are present in these variables despite lower outlier bounds being negative, so the anomaly is specifically at the zero boundary rather than from impossible negatives.
Anomaly Patterns.business_relevance: These could be valid business records such as returns netted to zero, canceled invoices, or non-sales placeholders, but they also may reflect data quality issues depending on business rules. Downstream analysis should quantify zero frequency, decide whether zero records belong in revenue/profit modeling, and possibly exclude them from analyses intended to represent completed sales.

Anomaly Patterns.pattern_name: Retailer ID is highly concentrated at a single value
Anomaly Patterns.description: Retailer ID appears unusually concentrated, with the middle 50% of records all sharing the same identifier.
Anomaly Patterns.evidence: For Retailer ID, q25 = 1185732.0, median = 1185732.0, and q75 = 1185732.0, so the entire interquartile range collapses to one value. Mean is 1173849.72, min is 1128299.0, max is 1197831.0, and skewness is -1.0805, showing a concentrated distribution with a left tail.
Anomaly Patterns.business_relevance: Because Retailer ID is an identifier rather than a continuous measure, this is not a numeric outlier issue but a distribution anomaly that may indicate one retailer dominates the dataset or that records are unevenly sampled. This is likely a real composition issue rather than a data error. Downstream analysis should account for retailer imbalance, avoid interpreting ID numerically, and consider stratifying or weighting by retailer.

Anomaly Patterns.description: Price per Unit has a relatively stable distribution overall, but a small set of records fall outside the typical price band.
Anomaly Patterns.evidence: Only 81 outliers were flagged (0.8396%), the lowest among the measured business metrics except Operating Margin. The typical range is bounded by q25 = 35.0 and q75 = 55.0, with IQR outlier limits of 5.0 and 85.0. Actual values range from 7.0 to 110.0, so the anomaly is confined to the upper tail above 85. Mean is 45.22 and median is 45.0, indicating little central distortion. Skewness is 0.3624 and kurtosis is 0.4302, both modest.
Anomaly Patterns.business_relevance: This pattern is more likely to reflect premium-priced products or channels than broad data quality problems. Downstream analysis can usually retain these values, but price-sensitive models may benefit from capping or segmenting high-price records.

Anomaly Patterns.pattern_name: Operating Margin has a few boundary anomalies but is otherwise stable
Anomaly Patterns.description: Operating Margin is comparatively well-behaved, with only a small number of values outside the expected range.
Anomaly Patterns.evidence: 44 outliers were flagged (0.4561%). The IQR-based bounds are 14.0 and 70.0, while observed values range from 10.0 to 80.0. Mean is 42.30 and median is 41.0, showing limited distortion. Skewness is 0.2275 and kurtosis is 0.1753, both close to symmetric/light-tailed behavior relative to the other financial variables.
Anomaly Patterns.business_relevance: The small number of low-margin and high-margin boundary values could be valid exceptional profitability cases or possible calculation inconsistencies. Because the distribution is otherwise stable, downstream analysis should keep the values but validate margin formulas for records below 14 or above 70.
